title: Rough puff pastry
subtitle: A laminated pastry technique that lifts without a beurrage
excerpt: Rough puff pastry is a simplified laminated pastry that produces real, layered lift without the full croissant or classic puff technique. Chunks of cold butter are left visible in the dough and folded in with several turns, creating layers as they melt in the oven. The result is flaky and tender, suited to pie lids, turnovers, sausage rolls, and tarts.
sourceNotes: Rough puff pastry is a British home-baking standard documented in domestic cookery manuals from the nineteenth century onward. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, describes the technique. Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), gives the standard formula and folding method.

recipe.yieldDescription: enough for one large pie lid or 24 sausage rolls
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the finished pastry in a block, wrapped tightly. Defrost overnight in the fridge before rolling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 3 days ahead and refrigerate. The pastry relaxes and rolls more easily after 24 hours.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven when baking the finished pastry product

content.text: Rough puff is the shortcut to laminated pastry: cold butter cut into chunks and worked briefly into the dough, then given three folds with rests between each. The butter is never fully rubbed in; the visible pieces are what creates the layers. The technique is faster than classic puff and more forgiving than croissant, and produces pastry with real, visible flake.

items.prepNote: very cold, cut into 1 cm pieces

content.text: Combine the flour and salt in a bowl. Add the cold butter pieces and toss them in the flour, then use your fingers to roughly flatten each piece rather than rub it in. The butter should still be in irregular, coin-sized pieces. Add most of the cold water and the lemon juice and bring together into a shaggy dough. Add the remaining water a tablespoon at a time if needed. The dough will look rough and uneven; this is correct.

Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and roll into a rough rectangle, about 20 × 40 cm. Fold one third over the centre, then fold the other third on top (a book fold). Turn 90 degrees and roll out again. Fold the same way. Wrap in cling film and refrigerate for 20 minutes. At this stage the dough will look streaky with butter; you should see no butter streaks at the cut edge by the third fold.
Second and third fold, with rests

Repeat the rolling and folding twice more, with a 20-minute rest in the fridge between each fold. After the third fold, the pastry should be smooth, with a fine laminated texture visible when you cut the edge. Rest for at least 20 minutes before rolling to its final thickness for the recipe.

Rough puff bakes at 200 to 220°C fan. The high heat converts the water in the butter to steam, which puffs the layers apart. At lower temperatures the butter melts before the steam can act, and the layers collapse. Always bake from cold; if the pastry has softened, chill it again before the oven.
